title: Synchronization of Bloch Oscillations in array of parallel Josephson Junctions
authors: R. Shaikhaidarov, R. Hussain, V. N. Antonov, E. Il'ichev
abstract: We demonstrate the synchronization of current quantization in a parallel array of weakly coupled Josephson Junctions operating in the regime of the coherent quantum phase slip. The first quantized current step on the voltage-current characteristic of 69 parallel Josephson junctions, under microwave excitation at a frequency of f = 19.325 GHz, is observed at 426 nA. Experiments show that accuracy of quantization does not degrade with increasing number of combined Josephson junctions. This demonstration addresses the issue of quantized current amplitude requirements adopted for a practical quantum standard, while leaving the question of accuracy for further research. At present, the quantum current standard of accepted metrological accuracy in the triangle of electrical units is under development. The other two, the Volt and Ohm standards based on the Josephson and Quantum Hall effects, respectively, are already well established in metrology.
summary: The researchers successfully synchronized 69 parallel Josephson Junctions to produce a larger quantized current of 426 nA. This demonstrates that the current amplitude can be scaled up for metrological purposes without degrading the precision of the quantization, providing a path toward a practical quantum current standard.
main_problem: The challenge of scaling current quantization from single Josephson Junctions to larger, usable amplitudes (mA and above) for a practical quantum current standard without degrading accuracy.
main_result: Demonstrated the synchronization of current quantization in an array of up to 69 parallel Josephson Junctions, achieving a quantized current of 426 nA without loss of accuracy or scaling degradation.
method: Experimental measurement of voltage-current (I-V) characteristics and differential resistance maps (dV/dI) using a dilution refrigerator and microwave excitation on TiN-based Josephson Junction arrays.
model_or_system: An array of parallel, weakly coupled Josephson Junctions operating in the regime of Coherent Quantum Phase Slip (CQPS), where the system follows a Bloch Hamiltonian and utilizes a screening circuit with superinductance.
key_observables: Voltage-current (I-V) characteristics, quantized current steps (I_dc,n), and differential resistance maps (dV/dI).
important_parameters: Microwave frequency f = 19.325 GHz, quantized current 426 nA, junction size ~50 x 40 nm^2, and thermal noise current Delta I_T ~ 0.4 nA.
main_assumptions: The accuracy of the quantization is limited by thermal fluctuations in the screening circuit resistors, and the system operates in the regime where microwave frequency is much larger than the plasma frequency.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the JJ array photo, SEM image, and circuit diagram; Figure 3 displays differential resistance maps for different array sizes; Figure 4 shows normalized I-V curves for various array sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the metrological need for current standards, describes the CQPS-based JJ array platform and fabrication, presents experimental results on synchronization and scaling, and concludes with an analysis of noise-induced accuracy limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 3. The maps of the differential resistance dV/dI in ar- rays of a) 44, b) 25 and c) single parallel junctions. dc and ac currents are normalized by quantized current 2efN and 2ef respectively. The dV/dI is normalized by N; d) the slices of the maps a), b) and c) along Iac axis at the position of quan- tized current with n = 1, 2 and 3. Data for the array with 44, 25, 1 JJs are colored with red, green and blue correspond- ingly.

FIG. 2. Current quantization in a single JJ a) at 19.42 GHz, b) at 9.97 GHz and in parallel array of 69 JJs c) at 19.28GHz, d) 9.83 GHz. Horizontal guidelines correspond to multiple of I = N × 2ef (N = 1 in a), b) N = 69 in c), d)); e) Quantized current steps in parallel arrays of JJs normalized by 2efN. The I −V curves are shifted horizontally by 4 µV for clarity. There are extra horizontal guide lines n ± ∆I/2ef, where ∆I= 1 nA is the deviation of current from the quantized value at the tip of the blockade voltage.

FIG. 4. I −V curves in the arrays with different number N of JJs a) 69, b) 25, c) 8; d) I −V curves normalized by N. The color scheme corresponds to a), b), c). The reference I −V curve of single JJ is blue. The thermal noise current is shown by the dashed horizontal line of corresponding color. Across all arrays it has value ∆IT = 0.4±0.1 nA. The microwave is switched off.
